title: Integrals
subject: Mathematics

sections.title: Introduction
concepts: Differential Calculus is centred on the concept of the derivative; Integral Calculus is motivated by the problem of defining and calculating the area of the region bounded by the graph of functions.

If a function f is differentiable in an interval I, its derivative f' exists at each point. The functions that could possibly have given function as a derivative are called anti derivatives (or primitive) of the function.
The formula that gives all anti derivatives is called the indefinite integral of the function, and the process of finding anti derivatives is called integration.
Integration arises from two types of problems: (a) finding a function whenever its derivative is given, (b) finding the area bounded by the graph of a function under certain conditions.
These two problems lead to indefinite and definite integrals, which together constitute the Integral Calculus.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects indefinite and definite integrals.
key_points: Integration is the inverse process of differentiation.
Integral calculus was developed to solve problems of finding functions from derivatives and finding areas under curves.

sections.title: Integration as an Inverse Process of Differentiation
concepts: Integration is the inverse process of differentiation. Instead of differentiating a function, we are given the derivative and asked to find its primitive.
Such a process is called integration or anti differentiation.
Anti derivatives (or integrals) of a function are not unique. They differ by an arbitrary constant C called the constant of integration.
If F is a function such that (d/dx)F(x) = f(x) for all x in interval I, then for any real number C, (d/dx)[F(x) + C] = f(x).
The family {F + C, C in R} denotes all possible anti derivatives of f.
Functions with same derivatives differ by a constant.

definitions.definition: A function F is called an anti derivative of f if F'(x) = f(x) for all x in the domain.
The symbol integral f(x) dx represents the entire class of anti derivatives, read as the indefinite integral of f with respect to x.
f(x) in the expression integral f(x) dx.
x in the expression integral f(x) dx.
Any real number C, considered as a constant function, that appears in the general anti derivative F(x) + C.

key_points: d/dx[integral f(x) dx] = f(x) and integral f'(x) dx = f(x) + C: differentiation and integration are inverses of each other.
Two indefinite integrals with the same derivative lead to the same family of curves and so they are equivalent.
integral [f(x) + g(x)] dx = integral f(x) dx + integral g(x) dx (Property III)
For any real number k, integral k f(x) dx = k integral f(x) dx (Property IV)
integral [k1*f1(x) + k2*f2(x) + ... + kn*fn(x)] dx = k1*integral f1(x) dx + k2*integral f2(x) dx + ... + kn*integral fn(x) dx (Property V: generalization)

subsections.title: Some properties of indefinite integral
properties: (I) d/dx[integral f(x) dx] = f(x) and integral f'(x) dx = f(x) + C
(II) Two indefinite integrals with the same derivative are equivalent (differ by a constant).
(III) integral [f(x) + g(x)] dx = integral f(x) dx + integral g(x) dx
(IV) For any real number k, integral k f(x) dx = k integral f(x) dx
(V) Generalization: integral [k1*f1(x) + k2*f2(x) + ... + kn*fn(x)] dx = k1 integral f1(x) dx + ... + kn integral fn(x) dx

sections.title: Methods of Integration
concepts: When integrals cannot be found by inspection, we use additional techniques: (1) Integration by Substitution, (2) Integration using Partial Fractions, (3) Integration by Parts.

subsections.title: Integration by substitution
concepts: The integral f(x) dx can be transformed by substituting x = g(t), so that dx = g'(t) dt, giving integral f(x) dx = integral f(g(t)) g'(t) dt.
Usually, we substitute for a function whose derivative also occurs in the integrand.

formulas.name: Tangent integral
formulas.formula: integral tan x dx = log |sec x| + C

formulas.name: Cotangent integral
formulas.formula: integral cot x dx = log |sin x| + C

formulas.name: Secant integral
formulas.formula: integral sec x dx = log |sec x + tan x| + C

formulas.name: Cosecant integral
formulas.formula: integral cosec x dx = log |cosec x - cot x| + C

subsections.title: Integration using trigonometric identities
concepts: When the integrand involves trigonometric functions, we use known identities to simplify before integrating.
cos 2x = 2cos^2 x - 1 gives cos^2 x = (1 + cos 2x)/2
cos 2x = 1 - 2sin^2 x gives sin^2 x = (1 - cos 2x)/2
sin x cos y = (1/2)[sin(x+y) + sin(x-y)]
sin 3x = 3 sin x - 4 sin^3 x gives sin^3 x = (3 sin x - sin 3x)/4

sections.title: Integrals of Some Particular Functions
concepts: Important standard formulae used for integrating many other related standard integrals.
key_points: (7) To find integral dx/(ax^2 + bx + c), write ax^2+bx+c = a[(x+b/2a)^2 + (c/a - b^2/4a^2)] and reduce to standard form.
(8) To find integral dx/sqrt(ax^2 + bx + c), proceed similarly as in (7).
(9) To find integral (px+q)/(ax^2+bx+c) dx, express px+q = A d/dx(ax^2+bx+c) + B = A(2ax+b) + B, then solve for A, B.
(10) To find integral (px+q)/sqrt(ax^2+bx+c) dx, proceed as in (9) and reduce to standard forms.

sections.title: Integration by Partial Fractions
concepts: A rational function P(x)/Q(x) where degree P < degree Q is called proper; otherwise improper.
Improper rational functions are reduced to proper by long division: P(x)/Q(x) = T(x) + P1(x)/Q(x).
Proper rational functions with factorable denominators can be decomposed into partial fractions.

definitions.definition: P(x)/Q(x) where degree of P(x) is less than degree of Q(x).
P(x)/Q(x) where degree of P(x) is greater than or equal to degree of Q(x).

formulas.name: Distinct linear factors
formulas.notes: Two distinct linear factors in denominator

formulas.name: Three distinct linear factors
formulas.notes: Three distinct linear factors in denominator

formulas.name: Repeated and distinct linear factors
formulas.notes: One repeated and one distinct linear factor

formulas.name: Linear and irreducible quadratic factors
formulas.notes: Where x² + bx + c cannot be factorised further

key_points: To find constants A, B, C, equate numerators after multiplying both sides by the denominator, then compare coefficients or substitute suitable values of x.

sections.title: Integration by Parts
concepts: Integration by parts is used for integrating products of functions.
Derived from the product rule of differentiation: d/dx(uv) = u dv/dx + v du/dx.
The integral of the product of two functions = (first function) x (integral of second function) - Integral of [(differential coefficient of first function) x (integral of second function)].

Usually, if any function is a power of x or a polynomial in x, we take it as the first function. In cases where the other function is inverse trigonometric or logarithmic, we take them as first function.
ILATE rule: Inverse trigonometric, Logarithmic, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential - priority order for choosing the first function.
key_points: Integration by parts is not applicable to all product functions. For example, integral sqrt(x) sin x dx cannot be solved this way.
When finding the integral of the second function, do not add constant of integration - it is superfluous.
integral log x dx = x log x - x + C (taking log x as first function and 1 as second)
integral e^x sin x dx = e^x(sin x - cos x)/2 + C
integral e^x cos x dx = e^x(sin x + cos x)/2 + C

subsections.title: Integral of the type e^x[f(x) + f'(x)] dx
subsections.formula: integral e^x [f(x) + f'(x)] dx = e^x f(x) + C
subsections.proof: Split into integral e^x f(x) dx + integral e^x f'(x) dx. Apply integration by parts to the first integral taking f(x) as first function and e^x as second. The terms cancel to give e^x f(x) + C.

formulas.name: Square root of x^2 - a^2
formulas.formula: integral sqrt(x^2 - a^2) dx = (x/2) sqrt(x^2 - a^2) - (a^2/2) log |x + sqrt(x^2 - a^2)| + C

formulas.name: Square root of x^2 + a^2
formulas.formula: integral sqrt(x^2 + a^2) dx = (x/2) sqrt(x^2 + a^2) + (a^2/2) log |x + sqrt(x^2 + a^2)| + C

formulas.name: Square root of a^2 - x^2
formulas.formula: integral sqrt(a^2 - x^2) dx = (x/2) sqrt(a^2 - x^2) + (a^2/2) sin^(-1)(x/a) + C

sections.title: Definite Integral
concepts: The definite integral has a unique value, denoted by integral from a to b of f(x) dx.
a is called the lower limit and b is called the upper limit of the integral.
The definite integral is introduced either as the limit of a sum or as the difference F(b) - F(a) where F is an anti derivative of f on [a,b].

definitions.definition: If f has an anti derivative F on [a, b], then integral from a to b of f(x) dx = F(b) - F(a).
The value a in integral from a to b of f(x) dx.
The value b in integral from a to b of f(x) dx.

sections.title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
concepts: The area function A(x) = integral from a to x of f(t) dt represents the area of the region bounded by the curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and the ordinates x = a and x.

definitions.definition: A(x) = integral from a to x of f(x) dx, representing the area under the curve from a to x.

theorems.name: First Fundamental Theorem of Integral Calculus
theorems.statement: Let f be a continuous function on the closed interval [a, b] and let A(x) be the area function. Then A'(x) = f(x), for all x in [a, b].

theorems.name: Second Fundamental Theorem of Integral Calculus
theorems.statement: Let f be continuous function defined on the closed interval [a, b] and F be an anti derivative of f. Then integral from a to b of f(x) dx = [F(x)] from a to b = F(b) - F(a).

key_points: The value of integral from a to b of f(x) dx = (value of the anti derivative F of f at the upper limit b) - (value of the same anti derivative at the lower limit a).
The arbitrary constant C disappears in evaluating the definite integral.
The function f needs to be well defined and continuous in [a, b].
Steps for calculating: (i) Find the indefinite integral F(x). (ii) Evaluate F(b) - F(a).

subsections.title: First fundamental theorem of integral calculus
Second fundamental theorem of integral calculus

sections.title: Evaluation of Definite Integrals by Substitution
concepts: To evaluate definite integrals by substitution, the steps are: (1) Consider the integral without limits and substitute y = f(x) or x = g(y) to reduce to a known form. (2) Integrate with respect to the new variable. (3) Resubstitute in terms of the original variable. (4) Find values at the given limits and compute the difference.
Alternatively, after performing steps 1 and 2, keep the integral in the new variable and change the limits accordingly, then evaluate directly.
key_points: When changing variable, the limits of integration must also be changed to correspond to the new variable.

sections.title: Some Properties of Definite Integrals
concepts: These properties are useful in evaluating definite integrals more easily.

formulas.name: Property P0
formulas.description: The variable of integration is a dummy variable.

formulas.name: Property P2
formulas.description: Splitting the interval at an intermediate point c.

formulas.name: Property P4
formulas.description: Particular case of P3 with lower limit 0.

formulas.name: Property P6
formulas.description: Useful for symmetric/antisymmetric functions about x = a.

formulas.name: Property P7
formulas.description: Even and odd function properties for symmetric intervals.

key_points: P4 is a particular case of P3 (with a = 0).
These properties are extensively used in competitive exams to simplify definite integrals.

summary: Integration is the inverse process of differentiation. Let (d/dx)F(x) = f(x). Then integral f(x) dx = F(x) + C. These are called indefinite integrals or general integrals. C is called constant of integration. All these integrals differ by a constant.

Some properties of indefinite integrals: (1) integral [f(x) + g(x)] dx = integral f(x) dx + integral g(x) dx. (2) For any real number k, integral k f(x) dx = k integral f(x) dx. More generally, integral [k1 f1(x) + k2 f2(x) + ... + kn fn(x)] dx = k1 integral f1(x) dx + ... + kn integral fn(x) dx.

Some standard integrals: (i) integral x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C, n != -1. (ii) integral cos x dx = sin x + C. (iii) integral sin x dx = -cos x + C. (iv) integral sec^2 x dx = tan x + C. (v) integral cosec^2 x dx = -cot x + C. (vi) integral sec x tan x dx = sec x + C. (vii) integral cosec x cot x dx = -cosec x + C. (viii) integral dx/sqrt(1-x^2) = sin^(-1) x + C. (ix) integral dx/sqrt(1-x^2) = -cos^(-1) x + C. (x) integral dx/(1+x^2) = tan^(-1) x + C. (xi) integral dx/(1+x^2) = -cot^(-1) x + C. (xii) integral e^x dx = e^x + C. (xiii) integral a^x dx = a^x/log a + C. (xiv) integral (1/x) dx = log |x| + C.

Integration by partial fractions: P(x)/Q(x) where P, Q are polynomials and Q(x) != 0. If degree of P >= degree of Q, divide to get T(x) + P1(x)/Q(x). Decompose into partial fractions of five standard types based on the nature of factors in denominator.

Integration by substitution: A change in the variable often reduces an integral to one of the fundamental integrals. Using substitution technique for trigonometric functions: (i) integral tan x dx = log |sec x| + C. (ii) integral cot x dx = log |sin x| + C. (iii) integral sec x dx = log |sec x + tan x| + C. (iv) integral cosec x dx = log |cosec x - cot x| + C.

Integrals of some special functions: (i) integral dx/(x^2 - a^2) = (1/2a) log |(x-a)/(x+a)| + C. (ii) integral dx/(a^2 - x^2) = (1/2a) log |(a+x)/(a-x)| + C. (iii) integral dx/(x^2 + a^2) = (1/a) tan^(-1)(x/a) + C. (iv) integral dx/sqrt(x^2 - a^2) = log |x + sqrt(x^2 - a^2)| + C. (v) integral dx/sqrt(a^2 - x^2) = sin^(-1)(x/a) + C. (vi) integral dx/sqrt(x^2 + a^2) = log |x + sqrt(x^2 + a^2)| + C.

Integration by parts: integral f1(x) f2(x) dx = f1(x) integral f2(x) dx - integral [f1'(x) integral f2(x) dx] dx. The integral of product of two functions = first function x integral of second - integral of (derivative of first x integral of second). Care must be taken in choosing first and second functions (ILATE rule).
Special formula: integral e^x [f(x) + f'(x)] dx = e^x f(x) + C.

Some special types of integrals: (i) integral sqrt(x^2 - a^2) dx = (x/2)sqrt(x^2 - a^2) - (a^2/2) log |x + sqrt(x^2 - a^2)| + C. (ii) integral sqrt(x^2 + a^2) dx = (x/2)sqrt(x^2 + a^2) + (a^2/2) log |x + sqrt(x^2 + a^2)| + C. (iii) integral sqrt(a^2 - x^2) dx = (x/2)sqrt(a^2 - x^2) + (a^2/2) sin^(-1)(x/a) + C.
Integrals of the types integral dx/(ax^2+bx+c) or integral dx/sqrt(ax^2+bx+c) can be transformed into standard form by expressing ax^2+bx+c = a[(x+b/2a)^2 + (c/a - b^2/4a^2)].

Integrals of the types integral (px+q)/(ax^2+bx+c) dx or integral (px+q)/sqrt(ax^2+bx+c) dx can be transformed by expressing px+q = A(d/dx)(ax^2+bx+c) + B, where A and B are determined by comparing coefficients.

We have defined integral from a to b of f(x) dx as the area of the region bounded by the curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and ordinates x = a, x = b. The area function A(x) = integral from a to x of f(x) dx leads to the Fundamental Theorems.

First fundamental theorem of integral calculus: Let the area function be defined by A(x) = integral from a to x of f(x) dx for all x >= a, where f is assumed continuous on [a, b]. Then A'(x) = f(x) for all x in [a, b].

Second fundamental theorem of integral calculus: Let f be continuous on [a, b] and let F be another function such that (d/dx)F(x) = f(x) for all x in the domain of f. Then integral from a to b of f(x) dx = [F(x) + C] from a to b = F(b) - F(a). This is called the definite integral of f over [a, b], where a is the lower limit and b is the upper limit.

Prove that: integral from 0 to pi/4 of 2 tan^3 x dx = 1 - log 2
Prove that: integral from 0 to 1 of sin^(-1) x dx = pi/2 - 1
integral dx/(e^x + e^(-x)) is equal to: (A) tan^(-1)(e^x) + C (B) tan^(-1)(e^(-x)) + C (C) log(e^x - e^(-x)) + C (D) log(e^x + e^(-x)) + C
integral cos 2x/(sin x + cos x)^2 dx is equal to: (A) -1/(sin x + cos x) + C (B) log |sin x + cos x| + C (C) log |sin x - cos x| + C (D) 1/(sin x + cos x)^2

If f(a + b - x) = f(x), then integral from a to b of x f(x) dx is equal to: (A) (a+b)/2 integral from a to b of f(b-x) dx (B) (a+b)/2 integral from a to b of f(b+x) dx (C) (b-a)/2 integral from a to b of f(x) dx (D) (a+b)/2 integral from a to b of f(x) dx
